title: A randomized controlled trial of mindfulness meditation for chronic insomnia
headline: A randomized controlled trial of mindfulness meditation for chronic insomnia
summary: A randomized controlled trial of Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction (MBSR) examined sleep. Post hoc analyses revealed that each intervention was superior to SM on each of the patient-reported measures, but no significant differences were found when comparing MBSR to MBTI from baseline-to-post. Mindfulness meditation appears to be a viable treatment option for adults with chronic insomnia and could provide an alternative to traditional treatments for insomnia.
keyFindings: Post hoc analyses revealed that each intervention was superior to SM on each of the patient-reported measures, but no significant differences were found when comparing MBSR to MBTI from baseline-to-post.
Mindfulness meditation appears to be a viable treatment option for adults with chronic insomnia and could provide an alternative to traditional treatments for insomnia.
limitations: Single-trial result; requires replication, and self-reported outcomes may inflate apparent effects.
The practice studied (Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction (MBSR)) is not the Miracle of Mind practice; findings do not transfer directly to MoM.
simplifiedTitle: A randomized controlled trial of mindfulness meditation for chronic insomnia
aiSnippet: RCT, Sleep (2014). Post hoc analyses revealed that each intervention was superior to SM on each of the patient-reported measures, but no significant differences were found when comparing MBSR to MBTI from baseline-to-post. Mindfulness meditation appears to be a viable treatment option for adults with chronic insomnia and could provide an alternative to traditional treatments for insomnia.
whyItMatters: MoM shares directed attention (the focused-attention component) in a brief, guided format with the practice studied here. Because the benefit is linked to that mechanism rather than to any one method, it's reasonable to expect it may extend to MoM as well — though MoM hasn't been studied directly for this outcome.
